title: Fruit consumption per capita
subtitle: Average fruit consumption per person, measured in kilograms per year.
caption:
Fruit consumption per capita, 2023

Average fruit consumption per person, measured in kilograms per year.

This choropleth map displays global variation in average fruit consumption per person for 2023, measured in kilograms per year. The color scale ranges from white (0 kg) to dark green (180 kg), with intermediate bands at 20 kg intervals. Highest consumption levels, in the 160-180 kg band, appear across the United States, Canada, and several countries in Central Asia including Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan. Western and Central European nations — France, Germany, Italy, Spain, and the Nordic countries — cluster in the 120-160 kg range. Australia, New Zealand, and parts of South America such as Chile and Argentina fall within the 100-140 kg band. In contrast, much of sub-Saharan Africa shows lower consumption: Nigeria, Ethiopia, and the Democratic Republic of Congo sit in the 20-60 kg bands. India and Indonesia are visible in the 60-100 kg range. The Middle East presents mixed patterns: Iran and Turkey reach 120-140 kg, while Yemen and Sudan remain below 60 kg. Greenland and some island nations carry hatched shading indicating no available data. The figures exclude international aviation and are based on per capita food supply at the consumer level, but do not account for food waste at the consumer level. Consumption levels show a broad geographic gradient, with temperate-zone economies generally occupying higher bands and tropical or low-income regions concentrated in lower bands. The darkest green areas — North America and Central Asia — represent the upper end of the global distribution, while large swaths of Africa and parts of South Asia occupy the lowest visible bands.
